title: Electrocatalytic Reduction of Dioxygen at the Surface of Glassy Carbon Electrodes Modified by Some Anthraquinone Substituted Podands
abstract:
Electrocatalytic Reduction of Dioxygen at the Surface of
Glassy Carbon Electrodes Modi®ed by Some Anthraquinone
Substituted Podands
Abdollah Salimi,+ Hossein Eshghi,++ Hashem Sharghi,++ Sayed Mehdi Golabi,+++ and Mojtaba Shamsipur*++++
+
Department of Chemistry, Tarbiat Modarres University, Tehran, Iran
++
Department of Chemistry, Shiraz University, Shiraz, Iran
+++ Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran
++++ Department of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran
Received: August 20, 1998
Final version: November 13, 1998
Abstract
The preparation and electrochemical characterization of glassy carbon electrodes modi®ed by some recently synthesized anthraquinone
substituted podands were investigated. These compounds were found to be strongly and irreversibly adsorbed on the preanodized glassy
carbon electrodes. The electrochemical behavior and stability of the modi®ed electrodes were studied by cyclic voltammetry in acidic
aqueous solutions. The in¯uence of pH on the electrochemical behavior of the electrodes was studied and a pH of 4.5 was chosen as the
optimum working pH. The ability of the modi®ed electrodes as potential electrocatalysts for the reduction of dioxygen to hydrogen peroxide
was tested by cyclic voltammetry, chronomperometry and rotating disk electrode voltammetry. They showed excellent electrocatalytic
ability for the reduction of O2 at pH 4.5 with overpotentials ranging from 380 to 470 mV lower than the plain glassy carbon electrode. The
heterogeneous rate constants for the reduction of O2 at the surface of these modi®ed electrodes were determined by the use of Koutecky-
Levich plots. In addition, the apparent diffusion coef®cients of O2 in buffered aqueous O2-saturated solution was estimated.
Keywords: Electrocatalysis, Glassy carbon electrodes, 1-Hydroxy-9,10-anthraquinone derivatives, Oxygen reduction
1. Introduction
The reduction of dioxygen to hydrogen peroxide or water
possesses an important role in electrochemical technology [1].
The involvement of dioxygen in energy conversion and storage
has led to a quest for newer catalysts for its electroreduction under
different experimental conditions. Modi®cation of the electrode
surfaces with electron transfer materials is a common approach to
preparing electrodes with electrocatalytic activity for the reduction
of O2. In this connection, a wide variety of compounds have been
proposed as electrocatalysts for dioxygen reduction, including
metal porphyrins [2, 3], metal phthalocyanines [4, 5], some
complexes of Cu2 [6, 7] and Fe3 [8] with chelating ligands,
macrocyclic Co2 complexes [9±11], anthraquinone polymers
[12, 13] and 1,4-naphthoquinone derivatives [14].
9,10-Anthraquinones as the largest group of naturally occurring
quinones are of fundamental importance both in industry and in
medicine [15±18]. Study of the electrochemical behavior of
different quinone derivatives has received considerable attention
during the past decade [19±25]. It is well known that, under dry
nonaqueous conditions, these compounds are characterized by two
one electron reductions [19, 20, 22, 24, 25]. The ®rst reduction step
to a semiquinone anion radical is generally reversible, while the
second step to a dianion is a nearly reversible or an irreversible
process, depending on the experimental conditions employed.
However, a single two electron reduction step is observed in the
presence of proton donors [21, 23]. The anthraquinone derivatives
have shown strong adsorption tendencies on various electrode
materials including Pt [26], Hg [27], graphite [28, 29] and glassy
carbon [30]. The enhancement of quinone-hydroquinone adsorp-
tion on glassy carbon electrodes after oxidizing treatment in a 1 : 1
concentrated nitric acid-sulfuric acid mixture has been reported by
Gomathi and Rao [31].
In recent years, we have been involved in the synthesis [32, 33],
acid-base [34±36] and electrochemical studies [35], and some
applications in extraction and membrane transport of metal ions
[37] and in construction of ion-selective electrodes [38] of some
new derivatives of 9,10-anthraquinone and 9-anthrone. In this
article, we report the use of six new anthraquinone podands (A1±
A6) [39] to modify glassy carbon electrodes. When adsorbed on
the surface of preanodized glassy carbon electrodes, the anthra-
quinones A1±A6 show enhanced catalytic activity for the electro-
reduction of dioxygen. We will discuss the ef®ciency of the
resulting modi®ed electrodes in the electrocatalysis of O2 reduc-
tion to H2O2 by cyclic voltammetry, chronoamperometry and
rotating disk voltammetry, and determine some diffusional and
kinetic parameters characterizing the electrode process.
2. Experimental
1-Hydroxy-9,10-anthraquinone derivatives A1±A6 (Fig. 1)
were synthesized [39] and used after recrystallization from
reagent grade benzene (Merck) and vacuum drying. HPLC grade
acetonitrile (Fluka) was used as received. Triply distilled deio-
nized water was used throughout. All chemicals used were of the
highest purity available from Merck or Fluka chemical companies
and used without any further puri®cation except for vacuum
drying. The buffers used at different pH values were 0.1 M
H2SO4  NaOH (for pH 0.5±1.5), 0.1 M H3PO4  NaOH (for
pH 2.0±3.0), 0.1 M acetate buffer (for pH 3.5±6.0) and 0.1 M
NaH2PO4  NaOH (for pH 6.5±13.0). N2 gas with purity
99.999 % was used to deaerate the solutions during the experi-
ments.
Electroanalysis 1999, 11, No. 2
# WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1999
1040±0397/99/0202±0114 $17.50:50=0
114
 The glassy carbon electrodes (GC, Metrohm, 0.031 cm2 area)
were polished with alumina powder (0.05 mm) for 5 min and then
washed with water. Preanodization of the GC electrodes was
performed by oxidizing the electrodes by potential cyclings
between 1.8 V and ÿ0.5 V at scan rates of 100 mVys in solutions
containing 0.5 M H2SO4 for 5 to 20 min, until a constant back-
ground was observed. The preanodized GC electrodes were then
placed in the deposition solutions (1.0610ÿ3 M anthraquinone in
acetonitrile) at open circuits for varying time periods (2±4 h) [40].
The resulting modi®ed electrodes were rinsed thoroughly with
water before subjected to the electrochemical measurements.
A three electrode system with a Pt wire counter electrode, an
AgyAgCl (saturated), 3 M KCl reference electrode and a modi®ed
GC working electrode was employed. The electrochemical mea-
surement were carried out at a thermostated temperature of
25.0  0.1 C. All voltammograms were recorded on a Metrohm
multipurpose instrument Model 693 VA processor, equipped with
694 VA stand and a thermal printer. A Metrohm drive shaft for
rotating disk electrodes was used. The pH measurements were
made with a Metrohm 632 pH meter using a combined glass
electrode.
3. Results and Discussion
3.1. Electrochemical Behavior of the GC Electrodes
Modi®ed with A1±A6
Glassy carbon electrodes bearing adsorptive ®lms of com-
pounds A1±A6 were prepared by electrochemical oxidation of
GC electrodes followed by immersing the electrodes in anthraqui-
none solutions in acetonitrile. Figure 2 gives the cyclic voltammo-
grams of the GC electrode modi®ed by compound A1 in 0.5 M
H2SO4 solution at various scan rates. A well-de®ned redox peak
corresponding to the reversible reduction of the anthraquinone was
observed. The formal potential of ÿ200 mV was obtained by
averaging the cathodic and anodic peak potentials. The ratio of
anodic to cathodic peak currents obtained at various scan rates was
almost unity. The anodic and cathodic currents increase linearly
with scan rate up to 800 mVys as shown in the inset of Figure 2.
The cyclic voltammograms were symmetric at ÿ200 mV and no
separation of peaks was observed at low scan rates (520 mVys). A
further increase in scan rate results in a separation of peaks (e.g.,
ca. 50 mV separation at 150 mVys).
Similar electrochemical behavior was observed for the case of
other anthraquinone derivatives used. The formal potentials
evaluated for compounds A1±A6 are listed in Table 1. As seen,
the formal potentials for all anthraquinone derivatives are closely
located around ÿ200 mV. This is most probably because the
polyether group substituted in 2-position of the mother anthraqui-
none is far from the electroactive center and, thus, the podand
chain length has no measurable effect on the formal potential of the
molecule [41].
The surface coverage G can be evaluated from the equation
G  QynFA
where Q is the charge obtained by integrating the cathodic peak
under the background correction (at low scan rates of 5±
10 mV sÿ1) and the other symbols have their usual meanings. The
calculated G values for all anthraquinone derivatives used are
also included in Table 1.
3.2. The Stability of the Modi®ed Electrodes
The stability of the modi®ed electrodes was studied by immer-
sing in acid and basic solutions for a long period of time and then
recording the cyclic voltammograms. The modi®ed electrodes
were quite stable in acidic solution so that after immersing in a
Fig. 2. Cyclic voltammograms for compound A1 adsorbed on a glassy
carbon electrode in a 0.5 M H2SO4 aqueous soltion at various scan rates.
The scan rates in mV sÿ1 are shown on the corresponding cyclic vol-
tammograms. The inset shows the plots of the anodic and cathodic
currents vs. scan rates.

none solutions in acetonitrile. Figure 2 gives the cyclic voltammo-

H2SO4 solution at various scan rates. A well-de®ned redox peak
corresponding to the reversible reduction of the anthraquinone was
observed. The formal potential of ÿ200 mV was obtained by

3.2. The Stability of the Modi®ed Electrodes
The stability of the modi®ed electrodes was studied by immer-
sing in acid and basic solutions for a long period of time and then
recording the cyclic voltammograms. The modi®ed electrodes
were quite stable in acidic solution so that after immersing in a
Fig. 2. Cyclic voltammograms for compound A1 adsorbed on a glassy
carbon electrode in a 0.5 M H2SO4 aqueous soltion at various scan rates.
The scan rates in mV sÿ1 are shown on the corresponding cyclic vol-
tammograms. The inset shows the plots of the anodic and cathodic
Fig. 1. Structure of the anthraquinone derivatives used.
Table 1. Formal potential, Ef, surface coverage, G, dioxygen reduction
peak shift, DE, and heterogeneous rate constant, k, for the electro-
catalytic reduction of O2 at the surface of GC electrodes modi®ed by
Dioxygen at Glassy Carbon Electrode Surfaces
0.5 M H2SO4 solution for 16 h there was only a slight decrease in
the corresponding cyclic voltammograms (55 %). On the other
hand, the loss of adsorbed species A1±A6 was more pronounced
when the electrodes were exposed to basic solutions. Although the
adsorptive anthraquinone species were continuously detached
from the electrode surface after prolonged soaking in basic
solutions, the very last few monolayers of the adsorbed species
were remained tightly bound to the preanodized GC surfaces.
These adsorptive species remained at the electrode even after brief
polishing with alumina on a polishing cloth; some times it required
extensive polishing with sand paper in order to remove the residual
adsorptive anthraquinone derivatives. This is most probably due to
the fact that the anthraquinone derivatives used were not only
simply adsorbed, but also trapped inside pores or crevices of the
GC electrodes. A similar stability was also observed for the
modi®ed electrodes under catalytic reduction of dioxygen.
3.3. Effect of pH on the Electrochemical Behavior of
Anthraquinones are known to undergo a two-proton two-
electron reduction in aqueous solution to give the corresponding
hydroquinone derivatives [21, 23]. Our results exhibited the
expected pH dependence of the voltammetric peak potentials.
Figure 3A shows the cyclic voltammograms of the GC electrode
modi®ed by anthraquinone A1 at different pH values. As seen, all
cyclic voltammograms possess one redox couple, the anodic and
cathodic peak potentials of which shift toward more negative
values by increasing pH of the solution. For all anthraquinone
derivatives used, the corresponding plots of formal potential
against pH resulted in a straight line (under pH of about 7.5)
with a slope between 61±64 mV per unit change in pH. A sample
plot is shown in Figure 3B. However, at pH values 47.5, slope of
the potential±pH plots reduced to a value of about 31±32 mV per
unit change in pH (see Fig. 3B) which is indicative of a two-
electron one-proton process resulting in the formation of the
corresponding semi hydroquinone derivatives. Moreover, as is
obvious from Figure 3A, the resulting cyclic voltammograms at
pH48 show a dramatic loss in the electrode's surface coverage
and poorly de®ned waves. This is most probably due to the
hydrolysis of the adsorbed anthraquinones at high pH values
3.4. Catalytic Reduction of O2 at the Modi®ed Electrodes
In preliminary experiments, it was found that the electrocata-
lytic reduction of dioxygen to hydrogen dioxide can successfully
occur at the surface of the GC electrodes modi®ed by anthraqui-
nones A1±A6 in acidic media. Although the reduction potentials
of both dioxygen and anthraquinones are pH-dependent [42], their
displacement may be unequal because of their different kinetic
behavior; thus, a pH of about 4.5 was found to be the optimum pH,
where the electrode's anodic wave completely disappeared and the
cathodic wave reaches its maximum value.
As an example, cyclic voltammograms at pH 4.5 of the GC
electrode modi®ed by anthraquinone A2 in the absence and
presence of O2 were compared with the corresponding voltammo-
grams at the surface of the bare GC electrode. Figure 4A shows
that the bare GC electrodes gives no peaks in deaerated solution,
whereas in the presence of dioxygen the O2 reduction occurs
irreversibly with a peak potential located at about ÿ1000 mV. On
the other hand, there is a large enhancement in the cathodic peak
current of the modi®ed GC electrode in the presence of O2 (Fig.
4B) while the corresponding anodic peak has disappeared clearly
indicating the electrocatalytic reduction of dioxygen at a peak
potential of about ÿ550 mV. The observed shifts in the reduction
of dioxygen at the surface of the GC electrodes modi®ed by
different anthraquinones used are also included in Table 1. It
should be noted that these potential shifts are larger than the
corresponding values reported for the catalytic reduction of
dioxygen at the surface of different modi®ed electrodes [2±14].
The scan rate dependence of cyclic voltammograms for GC
electrode modi®ed by anthraquinone A1 in a buffer solution of pH
4.5 saturated with O2 is shown in Figure 5. As the scan rate
increases, the catalytic reduction peak potential shifts slightly in
the negative direction and the anodic peak begins to grow. A
similar scan rate dependence was observed for other modi®ed
electrodes. Such behavior is most probably indicative of a kinetic
limitation existing in the reaction between the adsorbed anthra-
quinones and dioxygen. These voltammograms were used to
examine the variation of peak current against sweep rate. The
plots of the cathodic peak current, Ipc vs. the scan rate, n, shows Ipc
is linearly proportional to n1y2 (inset of Fig. 5), suggesting that at
suf®cient overpotentials the reaction is transport limited [43, 44].
Such behavior has also been reported for some previously
Fig. 3. A) Cyclic voltammograms for the glassy carbon electrode
modi®ed by compound A1 at a scan rate of 200 mV sÿ1 at different pH
values. The pH values are indicated on the corresponding cyclic vol-
tammograms. B) pH dependence of the formal potential for compound
A1 adsorbed on a glassy carbon electrode.
prepared modi®ed electrodes [14, 43±45]. The results obtained
thus far indicate that the overall electrochemical reduction of O2 at
the modi®ed electrodes introduced might be controlled by the
diffusion of O2 in solution and by the cross-exchange process
between O2 and the redox sites of the anthraquinone derivatives
The chronoamperometric behavior of bare and modi®ed GC
electrodes by compounds A1±A6 was examined in the absence
and presence of dioxygen. As an example, the chronoampero-
grams of bare and modi®ed GC electrodes by compound A4 in
deoxygenated buffered solution (pH 4.5) are illustrated in Figure
6A. The net electrolysis current was obtained by subtracting the
background current using the point-by-point subtraction method.
A plot of net current against tÿ1y2 shows a straight line (Fig. 6C,
curve 1) which extrapolates close to the origin. Since the oxidized
and reduced forms of the anthraquinone derivatives are known to
be insoluble in water, such a near-Cottrellian behavior may be
explained by ®nite diffusion in thin ®lms [14, 45]. On the other
hand, the corresponding chronoamperagrams for the same electro-
des in the presence of O2 were obtained at a reduction potential of
ÿ700 mV and the results are shown in Figure 6B. The correspond-
ing plot of net current vs. tÿ1y2 (Fig. 6C, curve 2) is linear at short
time periods, while it deviates from linearly at longer times. The
extrapolation of the linear part of the I vs. t1y2 plot will result in an
intercept at about ÿ8 mA. Since the direct reduction of O2 on a
plain GC electrode shows only a residual current, the transient
current seems to be largely due to the catalytic reduction of O2 by
the anthraquinone derivative used. As the electrolysis potential
steps to zero volt, no signi®cant current was observed (by
comparison of curves 10A and 10B in Fig. 6). This is indicative
of the irreversible nature the catalytic reduction of dioxygen [14].
Fig. 5. Cyclic voltammograms of a glassy carbon electrode modi®ed
with compound A1 in a buffered (pH 4.5) O2-saturated aqueous solution
at various scan rates. The scan rates in mV sÿ1 are indicated on the
corresponding cyclic voltammograms. The inset shows the correspond-
Fig. 6. Current±time curves obtained by the double potential-step
technique at an initial potential of ÿ700 mV and ®nal potential of
200 mV (vs. AgyAgCl (saturated), 3 MKCl). A) 1,10 glassy carbon
electrode modi®ed with A3 in the absence of O2 ; 2,20 as 1,10 for the
plain glassy carbon electrode. B) 1,10 glassy carbon electrode modi®ed
with A3 in a buffered (pH 4.5) O2-saturated solution; 2,20 as 1,10 for the
plain glassy carbon electrode. C) plots of net current vs. tÿ1y2 for the
glassy carbon electrode modi®ed with A3 in the absence (1) and pre-
Fig. 4. A) Cyclic voltammograms for a plain glassy carbon electrode in
a buffer solution of pH 4.5 at a scan rate of 10 mV sÿ1 in the absence (1)
and presence (2) of O2. B) Same as (A) for a glassy carbon electrode
Dioxygen at Glassy Carbon Electrode Surfaces
3.6. Kinetics of the Electrocatalytic Reduction of
It should be dif®cult to analyze the electrocatalytic process of
O2 on the modi®ed electrodes used by cyclic voltammetry alone,
because the rate-controlled process is complicated. However, by
using the rotating disk electrodes and keeping the electrodes at a
stationary potential, a steady state is attained. Thus, the rates of
mass transfer at the surface of the rotating disk electrode would be
much larger than those of charge transfer. Consequently, the
relative contribution of the effect of mass transfer to the electron
transfer kinetics will be smaller.
Thus, the catalytic reduction of O2 was studied with the GC
rotating electrodes modi®ed by compounds A1±A6 to obtain the
kinetic parameters. As an example, in Figure 7A are shown a set of
current±potential curves recorded in an O2 saturated buffered
solution (pH 4.5) at various angular velocities, o, with a rotating
disk GC electrode on which 2.9610ÿ9 molycm2 of anthraquinone
A1 was adsorbed. Curve a is the response of the modi®ed electrode
in the absence of dioxygen. The limiting current, Il, is de®ned as
the difference between the currents on a modi®ed electrode at the
potential corresponding to the diffusion plateau in deaerated and
O2-saturated solutions. The Levich [46] and Koutecky-Levich
[47] plots were prepared from the current±potential curves at
various angular velocities and are shown in Figures 7B and C,
respectively. The Levich plot in Figure 7B is very close to the
calculated line for a two-electron process (n  2) and shows some
negative deviation at higher o values. Such nonlinearity is not
unexpected for a catalyzed reduction in which a current limiting
chemical step precedes the electron transfer [6]. Similar results
have also been reported in the literature [6, 8, 14]. However, the
corresponding Koutecky-Levich plot (Fig. 7C) is linear, with a
slope close to that of the calculated line for the reduction of O2 via
two electrons. This is a good indication for the accomplishment of
the reduction of O2 to H2O2 by the adsorbed catalyst. A similar
behavior was observed in the case of other anthraquinone
The catalytic reaction rate between the reduced anthraquinone
AH2 and O2 (AH2  O2 ! H2O2  A) can be obtained from the
Koutecky-Levich plot using the following expression:

where CO2 is the bulk concentration of O2, o is the rotational
speed, n is the hydrodynamic viscosity, G is the surface coverage
of the corresponding anthraquinone, k is the rate constant and all
other parameters have their usual meanings. The rate constants
for the reduction of dioxygen were evaluated from the intercepts

The Tafel plot of E vs. ln (IyIl ÿ I) for all modi®ed GC
electrodes at different rotation rates were all quite linear with the
slopes between 60 and 65 mV. This is indicative of a redox
catalytic process, as was pointed out before [48, 49].
The diffusion coef®cient of O2 in buffered aqueous O2-
saturated solution (pH 4.5) was determined as (1.64  0.16)6
10ÿ5 cm2 sÿ1 using the Levich equation at various rotating
speeds of the rotating GC electrode. The values of kinematic
viscosity of water, n, and the concentration of O2 in solution, CO2,
used in this calculation were 0.01 cm2 sÿ1 and 1.25 mM, respec-
tively [50, 51]. The value obtained for the diffusion coef®cient of
O2 is comparable with the reported values [14, 52].
Some new 1-hydroxy-9,10-anthraquinone substituted podands
(A1±A6) were used to prepare modi®ed GC electrodes for the
electrocatalytic reduction of O2 to H2O2. The anthraquinones A1±
A6 were strongly and irreversibly adsorbed on preanodized glassy
carbon surfaces. Such anthraquinone-adsorbed electrodes could be
prepared easily and showed near Nernstian response with changes
in solution pH. The cyclic voltammograms of the resulting
modi®ed electrodes indicated the presence of a single couple
redox reaction (two-electron, two-proton) with formal potential
about ÿ200 mV with respect to AgyAgCl (saturated), 3 M KCl.
